cityCenter.name: Plaça de Catalunya
cityCenter.description: Barcelona's central hub where the Eixample meets the old town and La Rambla

routes.name: Aerobús (A1/A2)

price.note: ~€7.25 single, small discount on returns bought online; fares creep up yearly—check aerobusbarcelona.es

routes.destination: Plaça de Catalunya (via Plaça d'Espanya and Gran Via)
routes.tips: The default for most visitors: A1 serves T1 and A2 serves T2—board the right one. Dedicated luggage racks, dependable frequency, and it drops you dead-centre at Plaça de Catalunya. Buy on board by card or online in advance.
pros: Direct to Plaça de Catalunya with no transfers
Very frequent, with proper luggage space
Runs earlier and later than the metro on most days
cons: Gran Via traffic can add time at rush hour
Queues at the Plaça de Catalunya stop on busy mornings

routes.name: Metro L9 Sud

price.note: ~€5.50 special airport fare—standard singles and T-Casual are NOT valid at the airport stations; Hola Bcn passes are. Check tmb.cat

routes.destination: Zona Universitària (change at Torrassa for L1 or Collblanc for L5 to the centre)
routes.tips: Stations at both T1 and T2, but the L9 Sud skirts the city rather than piercing it—you'll change at Torrassa (L1) or Collblanc (L5) for anywhere central, so budget close to an hour door to door. Best value if you've bought a Hola Bcn pass, which covers the airport supplement.
pros: Serves both terminals directly
Immune to road traffic
Covered by Hola Bcn passes—no extra fare
cons: No direct run to the centre—a transfer is always needed
T-Casual and standard singles not accepted at airport stations

routes.name: Rodalies R2 Nord

price.note: ~€4.90 single; unlike the metro, T-Casual IS valid here. Departs from T2 only—free shuttle bus from T1 (allow 10-15 min)

routes.tips: The sleeper pick if you land at T2 (or don't mind the free inter-terminal shuttle from T1): about 25 minutes to Sants and it continues to Passeig de Gràcia, right in the heart of the Eixample. Half-hourly frequency is the only real drawback.
pros: Cheapest rail option, and T-Casual works
Direct to Sants and Passeig de Gràcia
Fast—no road traffic
cons: T2 only; T1 arrivals need the shuttle bus first
Only every 30 minutes

routes.name: Taxi (black-and-yellow)

price.note: Metered, including the airport supplement; a minimum airport fare applies. Night and weekend rates slightly higher

routes.tips: Ranks sit right outside arrivals at both terminals with rarely more than a short queue. Barcelona's black-and-yellow cabs are metered and honest—expect ~€35-40 to the centre including the airport supplement. All take cards; ask for a receipt if you like.
pros: Door-to-door in as little as 20 minutes
Regulated meters, card payment standard
Great value split between 3-4 people
cons: Several times the public-transport fare
Ronda Litoral traffic at rush hour

routes.name: Cabify / Uber / Bolt

price.note: Fixed upfront pricing; can exceed the taxi fare when demand surges

routes.tips: All the big apps operate in Barcelona (often dispatching licensed taxis or VTC cars). Follow signage to the designated pickup zones. Upfront pricing is the draw, but the regulated taxi rank is frequently just as cheap and faster to board.
pros: Fixed price known before you ride
Pay and track in the app
Door-to-door
cons: Surge pricing after late arrivals
Pickup zones can be a walk from arrivals

routes.name: Direct bus (Sagalés)

price.note: ~€16 single / ~€25 return; cheaper booked online in advance

routes.tips: Girona is a budget-airline satellite a good 90 km from Barcelona—'Barcelona' on a Ryanair booking deserves a second look. The direct Sagalés coach to Estació del Nord is timed to flights and is the simplest way in. Alternatively, a local bus to Girona city plus the AVE gets you to Sants in ~38 minutes if timings align.
pros: Direct, timed to flight arrivals
Cheapest sensible way to cover 90 km
Estació del Nord is a short walk from the Arc de Triomf metro
cons: Long ride after a flight
Miss the coach and the wait can be significant

routes.name: Taxi

price.note: Long-distance tariff; agree or confirm the price before setting off

routes.tips: Only worth considering for a group with a very late arrival—it's a 90 km motorway run and the fare shows it. For most travellers the coach wins comfortably.
pros: Door-to-door
Available when coaches aren't
cons: Very expensive
Long motorway journey

routes.name: Direct bus (Plana / Vibasa)

price.note: ~€17 single; book online in advance where possible

routes.tips: Reus is the other budget-airline satellite, about 100 km southwest near Tarragona. Direct coaches to Sants station are scheduled around flights; from Sants you're one metro hop from anywhere. If no coach fits, local buses connect to Tarragona for frequent trains up the coast.
pros: Direct to Sants, the city's main transport hub
Timed to flight arrivals
Far cheaper than any taxi
cons: Roughly 1h30-1h50 of travel
Sparse service outside flight times

routes.name: Metro L3 / L5 (Sants Estació)

price.note: ~€2.65 single, or one ride of a T-Casual (~€12.15 for 10). Check tmb.cat

routes.destination: Anywhere central (L3 to Plaça de Catalunya and Passeig de Gràcia; L5 to Diagonal and Sagrada Família)
routes.tips: Arriving by rail is Barcelona's most civilised entrance: AVE and low-cost AVLO from Madrid in ~2.5 hours, plus direct TGV/high-speed services from Paris (~6h40), Lyon and beyond land you at Sants. Two metro lines run right beneath the station—L3 (green) covers Plaça de Catalunya and Passeig de Gràcia, L5 (blue) hits Diagonal and Sagrada Família.
pros: You arrive already in the city
Two metro lines directly below the concourse
No airport-style security or long transfers
cons: The concourse is busy—watch bags around the metro entrances
Rush-hour crowds on L3/L5

routes.name: Taxi

price.note: Metered; most central hotels within this range, small station supplement applies

routes.tips: The rank is right outside the main entrance on Plaça dels Països Catalans. After a long train ride with luggage, a ~€12 hop to the Eixample or Gothic Quarter is money well spent.
pros: Cheap ride—you're already central
No escalators or stairs with your bags
cons: Traffic around Plaça d'Espanya at rush hour
Queue at the rank after big AVE arrivals

generalTips: Skip singles unless you're taking one ride: a metro/bus single is ~€2.65, while the T-Casual (10 rides, ~€12.15, one person only) cuts that to about €1.20 a trip and covers metro, bus, tram, FGC and Rodalies within Zone 1. Heavy sightseers can compare the unlimited Hola Bcn passes (2-5 days), which also cover the airport metro. Fares adjust most Januaries—check tmb.cat.
Metro hours are generous: ~5:00am to midnight Sunday-Thursday, until 2am on Fridays, and all night on Saturdays. The Aerobús and the N-series night buses (Nitbus) cover the gaps.

Note the airport quirk: the T-Casual is valid on the Rodalies R2 Nord train to the airport but NOT at the two L9 Sud metro airport stations, which require a special ~€5.50 ticket (or a Hola Bcn pass).

Walk the Eixample: the grid with its chamfered corners is flat, legible and full of Modernisme—Plaça de Catalunya to Sagrada Família is a pleasant 35-minute architecture crawl. The Gothic Quarter is best navigated by getting slightly lost on purpose.

The red-and-white Bicing bikes are for residents only (annual subscription tied to Spanish ID)—visitors should use one of the many rental shops near the beach and Ciutadella instead; the seafront bike path is superb.

Keep phones and wallets zipped away on the L3 metro line and along La Rambla—Barcelona's pickpockets are skilled rather than dangerous, and simple front-pocket awareness defeats nearly all of them. Nothing valuable in back pockets or open totes, especially in station crowds.

Black-and-yellow taxis are plentiful, metered and hailable on the street when the green roof light is on; Free Now and Cabify book them by app. For day trips, Rodalies trains reach Sitges in ~40 minutes and FGC trains connect to the Montserrat rack railway.
